title: Arrays and Hashmaps Basics
description: Easy-entry coding interview cards focused on the first patterns that show up in coding challenges and technical screens.

cards.question: When should I think about using a hashmap instead of scanning an array every time?
cards.shortAnswer: When fast lookup matters more than preserving a simple linear scan.
cards.expectedAnswer: If I need to repeatedly check whether something already exists, find an index quickly, or count frequencies, a hashmap is often the right move. A plain array scan is fine for one pass or tiny input, but if the problem keeps asking for lookup by value, the hashmap usually reduces repeated work and makes the solution simpler.
keyPoints: frequency counting is a common hint
avoid repeated scans when the problem keeps asking the same lookup question
commonTraps: using a hashmap when a single linear pass is already enough
forgetting the problem may care about order
followUps: What is the time complexity tradeoff?
When does an array still make more sense?

cards.question: What is a frequency map and why does it show up so much in coding interviews?
cards.shortAnswer: It stores how many times each value appears, which makes counting and comparison problems much easier.
cards.expectedAnswer: A frequency map is usually a hashmap where the key is the item and the value is how many times it appears. It shows up often because many interview problems are really about counting, matching, or detecting duplicates. Once you turn the input into counts, the rest of the problem often becomes much simpler.
keyPoints: helps with duplicates, matching, and counting
commonTraps: thinking every hashmap problem needs a frequency map
building the map when simple set membership is enough
followUps: When would a set be enough instead of a frequency map?
How would you compare two strings with the same letters?
